title: Controlling How Fast Noise Is Added
concept: The noise schedule determines exactly how much noise gets added at each forward diffusion timestep, shaping the entire training process.

exercises.prompt: When you add a few drops of food coloring to water, what happens if you add more drops over time?

options.text: The water becomes more and more colored/mixed
The water stays exactly the same forever
The water instantly turns into a completely different liquid

exercises.explain: Right! Adding a little bit at a time gradually changes the water. Diffusion models add noise the same gradual way, and the 'noise schedule' controls how fast that happens.

cards.title: Someone's Turning a Dial...
cards.body: Imagine a machine that slowly buries a clear photo under static, step by step, until it's pure fuzz. Who decides HOW FAST the fuzz takes over? That's the job of the noise schedule — the secret dial behind every diffusion model!

cards.title: What Is a Noise Schedule?
cards.body: A noise schedule is a plan that says exactly how much noise to add at each timestep during the forward diffusion process. Some schedules add noise slowly at first and speed up later; others spread it out evenly. It controls the whole journey from clean image to pure noise.

cards.title: Steeping Tea
cards.body: Think of dropping a tea bag into hot water. If you dunk it fast, the water darkens quickly. If you let it steep slowly, the color builds up gradually. The noise schedule is like choosing how fast the 'tea' (noise) spreads into the 'water' (image) at every single step.

cards.title: Step by Step
cards.body: Say a model has 1000 timesteps. At step 1, the schedule might add just a tiny sprinkle of noise — the image still looks almost normal. By step 500, it's half-noisy. By step 1000, the schedule has piled on so much noise that the image is pure static. The schedule decides the exact amount added at every one of those steps.

cards.title: Myth: Noise Is Always Added the Same Amount Each Step
cards.body: Not true! Many schedules (like 'cosine' schedules) add noise more slowly at the start and end, and faster in the middle. The amount of noise per step can change a lot depending on the schedule design — it's not just a flat, equal sprinkle every time.

cards.title: Linear vs Cosine Schedules
cards.body: Two popular ways to plan the noise-adding journey:

left.text: Adds noise at a steady, constant rate — same amount added at every step, like walking at one fixed speed.

right.text: Adds noise slowly at the start and end, but faster in the middle — like easing into a run, sprinting, then easing out.

exercises.prompt: What does a noise schedule control in a diffusion model?

options.text: How much noise is added at each timestep
What colors the final image will have
How many images the model can generate
The name of the training dataset

exercises.explain: The noise schedule is exactly the plan for how much noise gets mixed in at each step of the forward diffusion process.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Every noise schedule must add the exact same amount of noise at every single timestep.
exercises.explain: False! Schedules like linear add a steady amount, but others like cosine change the pace — slower at the start/end, faster in the middle.

exercises.sentence: The ___ decides exactly how much noise is added at each timestep of the forward diffusion process.
exercises.explain: The noise schedule is the plan controlling the pace of noise addition throughout diffusion.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best describes a cosine noise schedule?

options.text: It adds noise slowly at the start and end, faster in the middle
It adds the exact same amount of noise at every timestep with no variation
It removes noise instead of adding it during training

exercises.explain: Cosine schedules ease in and ease out, adding noise faster only in the middle stretch of the process.

exercises.prompt: A friend says: 'The noise schedule doesn't really matter — as long as noise gets added eventually, the model will work the same.' What's wrong with this thinking?

options.text: Nothing — the schedule truly has zero effect on how training goes
The schedule shapes the entire training process, so a poor schedule can make learning harder or images noisier at the wrong pace
Noise schedules only matter for audio models, not images
Schedules only affect how the model is named, not how it trains

exercises.explain: The noise schedule is a core design choice — it shapes exactly how information disappears over time, which directly impacts how well the model can learn to reverse the process.
